did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning algorithms, specifically the Extreme Learning Machine (ELM), for the classification of melanoma skin cancer from dermoscopic images. It compares ELM against other ML classifiers like SVM, KNN, and Random Forest, achieving an accuracy of 97% with 11 features.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the application of the Extreme Learning Machine (ELM) classifier to dermoscopic images for melanoma classification. It details specific methodology including dataset augmentation (MED-NODE), feature extraction (ABCD, GLCM, statistical), and comparative validation against SVM, KNN, and Random Forest classifiers, reporting new accuracy results (97% with 11 features). Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE JEEIT), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Extreme Learning Machine for Melanoma Classification
journal: 2021 IEEE Jordan International Joint Conference on Electrical Engineering and Information Technology (JEEIT)
brief_summary: This study applies the Extreme Learning Machine (ELM) classifier to the binary classification of melanoma versus normal skin lesions using dermoscopic images. The dataset was augmented to 200 images (100 normal, 100 melanoma) sourced from the MED-NODE database and online repositories. Feature extraction involved combining ABCD dermoscopic parameters, Grey Level Co-Occurrence Matrix texture features, and statistical metrics, resulting in 11 total features. The ELM model was evaluated against Support Vector Machine (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), and Random Forest classifiers. The ELM achieved a classification accuracy of 97% with 11 features and 91% with the top 5 features, demonstrating superior speed and efficiency compared to the other methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 100 melanoma images sourced from the MED-NODE database and online repositories.

The comparator class in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 100 normal/benign images sourced from the MED-NODE database and online repositories. Note: While the paper mentions 'benign lesions' and 'normal mole' in the context of the dataset and related work, the specific experimental classes were 'Normal' and 'Melanoma'.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into 'Normal' (benign/normal mole) and 'Melanoma' (malignant/abnormal) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the aim is to classify dermoscopic images into two categories (normal and abnormal/melanoma). The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels using the Extreme Learning Machine (ELM) and compares it with SVM, KNN, and Random Forest. Performance is measured by accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV on a binary dataset of 200 images (100 normal, 100 melanoma).

datasets_used.name: MED-NODE Database and Online Repositories
datasets_used.description: A dataset of dermoscopic images sourced from the MED-NODE dermatology database and various specialized websites for melanoma skin cancer. The images were in JPEG format and underwent augmentation (reflection, rotation, translation) to balance the classes.

use.training: Used for training the Extreme Learning Machine (ELM), SVM, KNN, and Random Forest classifiers. 70% of the augmented dataset (140 images) was used for training.
use.testing: Used for testing the classifiers. 30% of the augmented dataset (60 images) was used for testing. K-fold cross-validation (k=5) was also employed to prevent overfitting.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To prevent overfitting during model evaluation.
To determine the optimal K value for the K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classifier.
To calculate and evaluate performance metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value (PPV), and negative predictive value (NPV).
To identify and rank the most significant features for the classification process.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV derived from confusion matrices, and model selection for KNN was based on the Calinski-Harabasz technique.

MED-NODE Database and Online Repositorie.dataset_description: Augmented dataset of 200 dermoscopic images (100 Normal, 100 Melanoma). Split: 70% training (140 images), 30% testing (60 images). Evaluation also used 5-fold cross-validation.

experiments.name: Classification with 11 Features
experiments.notes: ELM and Random Forest achieved the highest accuracy (97%). ELM was 120 times faster than Random Forest.

experiments.name: Classification with Top 5 Features
experiments.notes: Top 5 features identified by Random Forest: Energy, TDS, Entropy, Skewness, and Circulation. SVM maintained 94% accuracy despite feature reduction.

MED-NODE Database and Online Repositorie.notes: The paper reports that ELM achieved 97% accuracy with 11 features and 91% (specifically 91.04% in conclusion) with 5 features. The study highlights ELM's speed and efficiency compared to SVM, KNN, and Random Forest.

architecture_used.main_model: Extreme Learning Machine (ELM) with Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel (ELM-RBF)
architecture_used.pretraining: None (ELM is a single-hidden-layer feedforward neural network where input-to-hidden weights are assigned randomly and remain constant; only output weights are calculated analytically)
architecture_used.input_size: 11 features (derived from ABCD dermoscopic parameters, GLCM texture features, and statistical metrics) or 5 features (Energy, TDS, Entropy, Skewness, Circulation)

architecture_details.layers: Input layer, Hidden layer (single or multi-layer of hidden nodes), Output layer
architecture_details.weight_initialization: Input-to-hidden weights chosen randomly and remain constant during training
architecture_details.training_method: Analytical calculation of output weights (hidden-to-output) in one step

training_details.optimizer: Analytical solution (Moore-Penrose generalized inverse) for output weights
training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (standard ELM minimizes output error)
training_details.training_schedule: Single-pass training (weights tuned only one time)

classification_or_output_head.output_activation: Not explicitly reported (standard ELM uses linear or sigmoid output for regression/classification)

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not reported (ELM parameters depend on the number of hidden nodes, which was not specified)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Extreme Learning Machine with Radial Basis Function kernel (ELM-RBF)
preprocessing_techniques: Noise filtering using a 3x3 sliding window median filter
Z-score normalization of extracted features to eliminate outlier effects

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Reflection, rotation, and translation over X and Y axes were applied to increase the number of normal images to balance the dataset. The final augmented dataset consisted of 200 RGB images (100 Normal, 100 Melanoma).

conclusions: The Extreme Learning Machine (ELM) classifier achieved a classification accuracy of 97% using 11 extracted features (ABCD, GLCM, and statistical metrics) for distinguishing between normal and melanoma dermoscopic images.
ELM achieved an accuracy of 91% when using only the top 5 most significant features (Energy, TDS, Entropy, Skewness, and Circulation) identified by Random Forest.

ELM demonstrated superior computational efficiency, being 120 times faster than the Random Forest classifier (0.011 seconds vs. 1.33 seconds) while achieving the highest accuracy (97%) among the tested classifiers (ELM, SVM, KNN, Random Forest).
The study confirms that ELM is a viable, fast, and flexible alternative to traditional machine learning algorithms like SVM, KNN, and Random Forest for melanoma classification tasks.
SVM maintained a consistent accuracy of 94% regardless of whether 11 or 5 features were used, outperforming other classifiers in the reduced feature set scenario compared to literature benchmarks.
The authors acknowledge that further improvements could be made to enhance the performance of the ELM classifier.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small dataset of only 200 augmented dermoscopic images (100 normal, 100 melanoma), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the results.

The dataset was artificially balanced using geometric augmentation (reflection, rotation, translation) to increase the number of normal images, which may introduce bias and does not reflect real-world class distribution.

The dataset was sourced from the MED-NODE database and unspecified online repositories, raising concerns about data leakage if these sources overlap with standard benchmark datasets used in other studies.
The paper lacks external validation; results are reported only on the internal test split (30% of the augmented dataset), making it unclear how the model performs on independent, unseen populations.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance differences between ELM and other classifiers (SVM, KNN, Random Forest).

The study is limited to a binary classification task (Normal vs. Melanoma) and does not address the more clinically relevant multiclass problem of distinguishing melanoma from various benign lesions (e.g., nevi, seborrheic keratosis).

Feature extraction relies on hand-crafted features (ABCD, GLCM, statistical) rather than deep learning-based feature extraction, which may limit the model's ability to capture complex, non-linear patterns compared to modern CNNs.
The authors acknowledge that further improvements are needed and propose future work on larger datasets and mobile applications, indicating the current model is not yet ready for clinical deployment.

While ELM demonstrated superior speed (120x faster than Random Forest), the high accuracy (97%) on such a small, augmented dataset may be optimistic and prone to overfitting despite the use of 5-fold cross-validation.
